data.title: BXP: Development Risk Limits Upside (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of BXP (BXP) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 12% of their value. Ongoing concerns about long-term office demand and BXP’s capital spending have weighed on shares, and the company even cut its dividend to improve retained free cash flow. That said, its well-staggered lease maturities position it well to improve occupancy next year, despite long-term risks. I last covered shares of BXP in August, and since then, the stock has returned 11%, outpacing the market’s 7% gain. With shares now essentially at my $70 price target and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BXP to determine if shares remain a buy.

As you can see below, BXP is national office REIT, focused in urban centers, with 187 properties. Unsurprisingly given the firm used to be called Boston Properties, Boston is its primary exposure, followed by significant presences in NY, Washington, and some West Coast cities. Its portfolio should be relatively insulated from federal cutbacks in DC given its tenants are largely private sector or quasi-private entities like Fannie Mae.

BXP noted the West Coast is weaker than the East Coast and sees limited development opportunity in those markets. Salesforce (CRM) is an anchor tenant. Salesforce has announced plans to deepen its investment in San Francisco, which all else equal, should be a positive for BXP’s portfolio there. Financial services and legal services account for half of its portfolio, and these industries have had some of the strictest return-to-office policies. While 20% of the portfolio is leased to tech firms, even these firms are increasingly bringing workers back into the office. While remote working is likely to stay structurally higher post-COVID than pre-COVID, it simply is not the existential threat it appeared it might be four years ago, in my view.

In the company’s third quarter reported on October 28th, BXP generated $1.74 of funds from operations, which beat estimates by $0.02. Despite all of the long-term fears around office space, revenue was actually up 1.4% to $872 million. The company is benefiting from the nature of its renewal schedule, which should be a tailwind for results into 2027. Additionally, during the quarter, it leased 1.5 million square feet of space, about 30% more than its normal Q3. This points to favorable demand dynamics in the near-term, led by strength in Manhattan.

The company expects occupancy to end this year around 86.2% with about 200bps of improvement next year. That is because its leases are well-staggered with a ~7 year average life. Even more importantly, just 3.5% of leases expire next year. That creates a very low hurdle to re-lease space to actually increase occupancy, especially given the strong leasing seen in Q3, which will take time to convert into occupancy over 2026. Importantly, no individual lease expiring next year is more than 0.3% of the portfolio, so BXP does not have any anchor lease risk that could be difficult to replace. It already has 1.1 million of active proposals in the pipeline, covering about two-thirds of next year’s available space. Even if long-term office trends are challenging, the near-term trajectory is positive, just given the timing of its lease maturities.

While its occupancy trends should be a positive, its capital spending is a concern. As you can see below, the company has a material development pipeline, totaling $2.6 billion. This spending will peak next year at ~$750 million. This spending trajectory assumes BXP builds 343 Madison by itself, which would be a disappointing (and unlikely) outcome. By taking on a partner, BXP’s share of development costs should decline by about $800 million to $1.8 billion. Of that, it should raise ~$1.2 billion from asset sales, and its dividend cut should save the company about $600 million over the next four years. With these actions, BXP can finance its entire development pipeline without needing to incur additional debt.

Diving deeper into its planned capital raising, the company is focused on selling assets to finance growth projects, namely its new 343 Madison Avenue tower, a $2 billion project. It sold 4 land parcels for $57 million and has 9 assets under contract for $400 million. There are 10 more properties for sale that it expects to net at least $750 million from.

On its 343 Madison tower, the long-term plan is to reduce financing costs by bringing on a junior partner with a 30-50% equity stake in the building. While discussions have begun, it does not expect to sign a partner until next year. BXP wants to announce several leases, which should improve the valuation on the building, first before selling a stake. To that end, it has finalized discussions to lease 30% of the space to an unnamed financial services company. This area of Manhattan is a banking hub, just blocks from JPMorgan Chase’s (JPM) headquarters.

It is not all good news. While signing an anchor tenant would validate this investment, the rumors are the tenant will be insurer C.V. Starr. This entity is already located in BXP properties. As such, if it signs a new lease at 343 Madison, it is positive for that project but would not be a significant net positive for BXP unless it can re-lease vacated space at other buildings to new tenants. I expect its new lease to be accretive relative to existing leases, so this news is still a net positive but only incrementally so.

Succeeding on its capital raising is essential because BXP’s balance sheet is carrying significant debt already—about $16.6 billion. As you can see below, it has meaningful annual maturities that it will need to refinance. Given the relatively low cost of its borrowings, I expect refinancing to be a modest ongoing drag on FFO through 2030, barring a significant decline in interest rates. BXP’s net leverage is 8.2x, which is very elevated. I would like to see this metric move down over time. As it raises cash from asset sales and begins to generate EBITDA from development properties, I expect leverage to move towards a more reasonable 6x. This is likely to be a multiyear process not achieved before 2028 in my view.

Back in August, I was targeting $6.85-$6.90 of FFO. Alongside results, BXP increased guidance to $6.89-$6.92. Given the near-term visibility of rental revenue, guidance is quite credible, and BXP is on track to deliver results at the top end of my expectations. In 2026, I expect ~3% FFO growth thanks to improving occupancy, partially offset by refinancing costs. This points to about $7.15 of FFO. Having cut its dividend earlier this year, BXP shares now offer a ~4% dividend yield. I view this payout as sustainable with over 2x coverage, reflecting the need to retain cash for its capital program and debt reduction.

Shares currently trade at 10x FFO. Given its highly-levered balance sheet, significant execution risk around 343 Madison, and long-term growth concerns, I struggle to see a case for much further multiple expansion, especially with a dividend increase unlikely for several years. Plus, further weakening of the labor market would be an additional headwind for office demand. That said, a leasing announcement and successful stake sale in 343 Madison could support shares by de-risking its development pipeline. As such, at $71, I do not see a rush to sell but am downgrading the stock to a “hold” and would want a pullback into the mid-$60’s before adding. On rallies past $76, I would take profits.

data.title: SPGP: Why This S&P 500 GARP ETF Looks Better With Less Energy Sector Exposure (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the Invesco S&P 500 GARP ETF (SPGP) on May 1, 2025, when I issued it a "sell" rating due to its large disconnect between its historical and estimated earnings growth rates. Since that article was published, SPGP has lagged behind the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) by 5.54%, though it outperformed the Invesco S&P 500 ETF (RSP) by 3.75%. These results indicate how mega-cap companies continue to lead, while alternate weighting schemes, including the one employed by SPGP, struggle to keep pace, despite its growth focus.

After reviewing SPGP's current fundamentals, which reflect one of its semi-annual reconstitutions, I've decided to upgrade SPGP to a "hold." Although not intentional, the fund's future growth prospects have improved, and it's now on solid footing from both a historical and forecasted GARP perspective. In addition, SPGP has lower earnings risk than before, which I will demonstrate by examining its new sector composition. I hope you enjoy the read, and as always, I look forward to your comments afterward.

Invesco S&P 500 GARP ETF Overview

SPGP Strategy Discussion

Since June 21, 2019, SPGP has tracked the S&P 500 GARP Index, which launched only a few months prior on February 25, 2019. Previously, SPGP tracked the RAFI Fundamental Large Growth Index and the Russell Top 200 Pure Growth Index, so given these significant changes, it's inappropriate to consider SPGP's performance prior to the Index change. Therefore, the remainder of this article will focus on performance from July 2019 onwards.

The S&P 500 GARP Index holds 75 companies from the S&P 500 based on five screens. First, growth scores based on three-year historical sales and EPS growth rates are computed, with the top 150 proceeding to the next step. Next, these stocks are ranked based on their quality and value composite scores (financial leverage, return on equity, and earnings-to-price ratio), with the top 60 qualifying. Finally, the Index selects 15 more stocks from the remaining 90 based on their quality and value composite scores. Ultimately, all 75 stocks are weighted by their growth scores only, so although other factors are considered (value, risk, and quality), growth is the primary focus.

Specifically, it's backwards growth-focused, so disconnects may exist if growth rates have clearly peaked for a sector and are forecasted to trend downward. For SPGP, that meant holding Energy stocks even as inflation moderated, and one of the key composition differences now is that its Energy sector exposure has dropped from 23.39% in May to just 6.63% today. That's a big reason for my change in outlook, but still, it's concerning that a growth-focused strategy isn't designed to identify growth rate trends, and for Energy, they're pretty clear. To illustrate, consider the following growth statistics for the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Energy ETF (RSPG), and I think you'll quickly see how Energy stocks don't belong in any growth-oriented fund today.

SPGP Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

As of December 6, 2025, SPGP's top ten sectors were Industrials (22%) and Consumer Discretionary (21%), while it allocates only 1% to Materials and 0% to Utilities. However, when compared to a peer group that's comprised of SPY, RSP, the SPDR S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV), and the SPDR S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG), SPGP actually underweights Technology by 10% on average. This is slightly strange for a growth ETF, but it makes sense, given how Technology is considered extremely overvalued on both an absolute basis and relative to its own 15-year history, per State Street's October SPDR Sector Scorecard.

SPGP's latest top ten holdings are listed next. Currently, the ETF is led by Nvidia (NVDA), though its 3.20% allocation is smaller than what you'll find in most growth ETFs. For example, Nvidia has a 13.99% allocation in SPYG.

The reason is that SPGP is growth-score-weighted, and the relatively tight differences in weightings suggest the range of growth scores is fairly narrow. Notably, market cap isn't a factor. Consider that Nvidia has a $4.4T market cap right now, but Expedia (EXPE) and Delta Air Lines (DAL) are much smaller at $32.3B and $43.5B, respectively. Together, these features mean SPGP should help investors diversify away from mega-cap stocks, and that's reflected in its minimal overlap (5-20%), as highlighted in the following ETF Overlap analysis.

Invesco S&P 500 GARP ETF Analysis

SPGP Performance: July 2019 to November 2025

If shareholders had invested $10,000 in SPGP starting on July 1, 2019, their investment would be worth $21,813 on November 30, 2025, which is a 118% total return that works out to an annualized 12.76%. The result is great, but it also lagged behind SPY and SPYG by 3.04% and 5.49% per year, respectively.

SPGP edged out RSP and SPYV by an annualized 1.65% and 0.54% over this period, but interestingly, it did so with much greater volatility, as measured by its 19.54% annualized standard deviation. This figure was even greater than SPYG's, and unfortunately, led SPGP to lag behind SPYV on risk-adjusted and downside risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino Ratio).

SPGP Fundamental Analysis: Excellent Backward and Forward GARP Features

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for SPGP's top 20 holdings, which total 42.37% of the portfolio on a cash-adjusted basis. Please have a scan of these holdings and the summary comparisons at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

In May, SPGP's three-year historical EPS growth rate was 7.63%, while its one-year estimated EPS growth rate was even lower at 3.17%. That's because Energy stocks like EOG Resources (EOG), Marathon Petroleum (MPC), and Valero Energy (VLO) occupied the top three spots, but now that they're gone, those growth rates have dramatically improved to 19.20% and 14.63%, respectively. It's a massive change that makes SPGP look like an actual growth ETF once again, and although these growth rates are still below SPYG's, I think investors will appreciate its significantly lower P/E ratios.

To put this together, consider the following trailing PEG ratios for each ETF, calculated by dividing the three-year historical EPS growth rate into the trailing P/E ratio:

  • SPGP: 18.75x / 19.20% = 0.98x
  • SPY: 27.65x / 14.80% = 1.87x
  • RSP: 22.00x / 4.64% = 4.74x
  • SPYV: 22.38x / 4.21% = 5.32x
  • SPYG: 34.17x / 23.71% = 1.44x

I made these same calculations in May, and at the time, SPGP still had the edge over SPY (2.00x vs. 2.40x, 17%), but today's gap (0.98x vs. 1.87x, 48%) demonstrates a huge improvement. To be clear, that's what the Index is designed to do with each reconstitution, but I also noticed a big improvement using forward PEG ratios (forward P/E and one-year estimated EPS growth), and that's something the Index does not always achieve:

  • SPGP: 17.10x / 14.63% = 1.17x
  • SPY: 24.63x / 17.88% = 1.38x
  • RSP: 18.53x / 9.20% = 2.01x
  • SPYV: 19.65x / 10.59% = 1.86x
  • SPYG: 31.11x / 23.86% = 1.30x

Again, SPGP ranks #1 in this peer group on its forward PEG ratio, and its 1.17x figure today is a dramatic improvement over the 4.56x I calculated in May. In my view, it's the fund's biggest selling point right now.

SPGP Earnings Risk: A Noticeable Decline

According to its latest annual financial statements, SPGP's portfolio turnover rate was 69% for the fiscal year ending April 30, 2025, with turnover ranging from 49-68% in the four years prior. These turnover rates are reasonably high, so it's important that investors keep apprised of any big changes to the fund.

While monitoring every change is not necessary, sector composition changes can provide important clues about whether SPGP is at risk of getting trapped by holding stocks in sectors whose earnings have grown quickly but can also change just as fast. Specifically, investors should watch out for big allocations to Technology, Financials, and Energy because, according to this spreadsheet from S&P Global, these are the three sectors with the most quarterly earnings variability from Q1 2008 to Q2 2025.

I've summarized this in the table below. For example, the Energy sector has a 107% long-term coefficient of variation, indicating that its quarterly EPS has actually varied by more than its $7.34 EPS average since Q1 2008. This huge volatility is why SPGP previously carried huge earnings risk, but now that its allocation to Energy stocks is much lower, it should produce less volatile returns, at least in the near term.

The table also shows long-term and short-term (last twelve quarters) figures for SPY, RSP, SPYV, and SPYG, and I'm comforted that SPGP's results are in line with these benchmarks. Negative contributions to risk include its 15% allocation to Financials and 20% allocation to Technology, but again, it's not unusual, so I'm not concerned at the moment.

Investment Recommendation

I've decided to upgrade my rating on SPGP to a "hold" from a "sell" due to its substantially improved growth-at-a-reasonable-price and earnings quality features. Both of these improvements are related to SPGP's Energy sector exposure declining by about 15-16% since my May review, but unfortunately, there isn't anything stopping its Index from overweighting this sector again, and its lack of forward-looking screens means shareholders could get stuck holding stocks whose growth rates are trending downward. I believe GARP as a strategy has merit, but I don't like the way it's executed in this case, so I don't suggest treating it as a long-term hold. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market sentiment has improved, even as manufacturing data struggle to break out of contractionary territory, with consumer indicators stabilizing and inflationary pressures remaining contained amid tariff-related uncertainty. - Harbor Capital Advisors, Inc.

Market in Review

The third quarter of 2025 was defined by a resilient market rally, as volatility subsided and equity indexes reached all-time highs. The sustained momentum was driven by renewed optimism surrounding artificial intelligence ("AI") and increased commitments to capital expenditure spending from mega-cap tech companies, coupled with strong earnings. Investors were able to look past lingering macro-related risks, as fears of U.S. trade policy receded so far, amid a limited inflationary impact.

Although inflation remained largely contained, the labor market showed increasing signs of strain. Job openings declined, wage growth and hiring momentum slowed, and participation rates softened – pointing to a cooling beneath the surface of a resilient economy. In September, the U.S. Federal Reserve ("Fed") cut rates for the first time in over a year, deciding that the risks to growth and employment outweighed the threat of renewed inflation. Risk assets rallied on the news as officials opened the door to future rate cuts in 2025.

Equity markets delivered another strong quarter of performance during the quarter. Global equity indexes, measured by the MSCI ACWI, rose by 7.74%, showing broad gains across regions. In the U.S., large-cap returns remained robust, as the S&P 500 advanced 8.11% during the quarter. Meanwhile, small caps outperformed, as the Russell 2000® Index surged 12.39% due to supportive policy and renewed risk appetite. On a sector basis, Information Technology (13.04%) and Communication Services (11.82%) outperformed, benefiting from AI-driven investment. Consumer Staples was the only GICS sector to post a negative performance (-2.90%) during the quarter; however, all GICS sectors remained positive for the year ending September 30, 2025, demonstrating the market's underlying breadth.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter, the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners ETF ("ETF") returned 8.53% (NAV), underperforming the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index, which returned 8.57%, and underperforming its benchmark, the Russell 2000® Index, which returned 12.39%.

The ETF underperformed the benchmark due to an underweight position and negative stock selection in the Industrials sector. The ETF's holdings in the Materials sector were also disappointing, although this was partially mitigated by its above-benchmark weighting in the segment. Elsewhere, an overweight in Financials proved unhelpful during the quarter when this sector lagged the broader Russell 2000® Index. More positively, stock selection in the Consumer Discretionary sector, notably the Hotels, Restaurants and Leisure sub-segment, added value.

Outlook

Our Business Cycle Regime indicator continues to signal a late-cycle expansion, with global growth moderating but remaining resilient. Market sentiment has improved, even as manufacturing data struggle to break out of contractionary territory, with consumer indicators stabilizing and inflationary pressures remaining contained amid tariff-related uncertainty. Monetary policy has become more accommodative, as the Fed's decision to begin its rate-cutting cycle marked a key inflection point for risk assets. Markets currently anticipate one to two 25-basis-point cuts by year-end, leading to higher expectations for economic growth in 2026.

Within this late-cycle backdrop, we have upgraded small caps to overweight. Valuations appear more attractive relative to large-cap peers, as improving earnings quality and cash-flow generation leave small caps well positioned to benefit. Beyond company fundamentals, the macro environment also supports rate-sensitive assets, with a steepening yield curve signaling improving growth expectations, rising fiscal deficits providing liquidity, and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act's incentives stimulating investment and continued momentum.

While the combination of Fed easing and AI-driven optimism evokes memories of the dot-com era, when new technology and rate cuts fueled an equity market melt-up, the current rally is nuanced and built on a stronger foundation. Mega-cap tech companies lead investment with strong balance sheets and real earnings power. Still, parallels to the late 1990s are difficult to ignore — liquidity remains ample; valuations are elevated; and market narratives continue to drive capital in ways that have historically extended cycles but could also introduce potential fragility if conditions shift.

Beyond domestic equities, the outlook for emerging markets is positive, as better-than-feared growth in China, a weaker U.S. dollar, and easing financial conditions meet attractive valuations. With emerging market growth forecasts near 4% for 2025–2026 and improving global liquidity, we maintain a constructive view on risk assets. We believe gold continues to serve as a valuable portfolio diversifier should equity market optimism prove to be exuberant or inflation re-emerge.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

A basis point is one hundredth of 1 percentage point.

Alpha is the risk-adjusted excess return of an investment relative to the return of a benchmark index.

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation.

Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity.

The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

data.title: Asia's Growing Economic Power Shapes Global Derivatives Market
data.description:
By Russell Beattie

Asia Pacific is playing an increasingly important role in the global economy and is expected to contribute around 60% of global growth in both 2025 and 2026, according to the International Monetary Fund. Asian economies such as China, Japan, and India now account for three of the top five countries in the World Bank’s global nominal GDP rankings.

The combination of the region’s ongoing economic importance, developing financial markets, and regulatory environment is creating opportunities in derivatives, as the global market shifts its focus eastward.

Rising Derivative Demand

Asia Pacific accounted for the largest share of derivatives trading of any region during October 2025 at 62% of all futures and options traded globally during the month, with trading volumes in the region increasing by 4.5% month-on-month, according to the Futures Industry Association.

This regional momentum was also reflected at CME Group – global average daily volumes (ADV) hit their highest level for October on record at 26.3 million contracts, with ADV in Asia Pacific rising by a significant 29% year-on-year to 2 million contracts.

Growth in derivatives trading in Asia Pacific is being driven in part by its rising middle class. The number of middle-class consumers in the region has already overtaken the combined total for the U.S. and Europe, and it is projected to continue to grow to hit 3.5 billion by 2030, when it will account for 65% of the global total.

As retail investors become both more affluent and sophisticated, moving beyond savings accounts to diversified investment portfolios, many are looking for ways to manage the risks associated with interest rates, currency fluctuations and equity volatility.

At the same time, the growing importance that Asia plays in the global economy has led to increased demand from institutions, both locally and internationally, to manage a wider range of risks. This rising demand is expected to continue to drive the development of the futures and options market over the next five to 10 years.

One area that reflects this rising demand is Asian consumers’ growing appetite for gold derivatives. Gold has long been favored by Asian investors, not only as a symbol of wealth, but also as a perceived safe-haven asset.

Its appeal has grown as a combination of trade tensions, geopolitical conflicts and the outlook for the U.S. economy have contributed to increased global economic uncertainty.

Gold has enjoyed a significant bull run, with prices rising by more than 50% in the past year and doubling in the past five years, to reach a record high of nearly $4,380 per ounce in October.

In the second quarter of 2025, a third of CME Group Gold futures by volume were traded during Asian hours, up from around 25% historically, while Micro Gold futures volume rose to 42%.

China Continues to Embrace Derivatives Markets

The ongoing development of China’s derivatives market is a significant highlight in the Asia-Pacific derivatives industry. Over the past 15 years, China’s futures and options market has experienced robust growth, in terms of both product availability and investor access, as the market has developed in line with the real economy.

China’s regulators are continuing to open up the derivatives market to Qualified Foreign Investors (QFIs), helping to attract global capital, create robust price discovery mechanisms and support the internationalization of the renminbi.

A series of announcements this year saw QFIs gain access to 23 new commodities contracts in March and a further 16 contracts across three major exchanges in June. In October, participation in on-exchange ETF options was opened to QFIs for hedging purposes, bringing the total number of tradeable futures and options products available to QFIs to more than 100.

Meanwhile, in May, the Shanghai Futures Exchange announced a consultation on a draft of proposals that would enable increased participation from overseas investors, including allowing them to use foreign currency as collateral for yuan-denominated trades and to trade directly with the exchange without having to go through an onshore intermediary. It is also looking at opening its domestic nickel futures contract to foreign investors.

China is also continuing to develop the regulatory framework for its futures market. On October 9, 2025, the China Securities Regulatory Commission’s Administrative Provisions on Program Trading in the Futures Market (Trial) was implemented.

The provisions aim to enhance the regulation of program trading, standardize its development and maintain order in futures trading, and bring regulation in line with that governing the securities market.

Morgan Stanley became the second U.S. bank to have a futures operation in China after launching its service earlier this year, marking another significant development in the market.

In the coming decade, China’s futures market is not only expected to continue to develop, but it could also foster the growth of smaller, rapidly developing economies in the region, as China shares its professional expertise and its infrastructure projects strengthen connectivity with neighbouring countries.

Vietnam Introduces Reforms

Regulators in Vietnam are also taking steps to open its markets to foreign investors. A series of reforms in 2025 have included introducing a new stock market trading system to improve reliability and align it with global trading practices, removing pre-funding requirements for Foreign Institutional Investors and reforming benchmark interest rates.

Amid these changes, FTSE Russell has announced that it will upgrade Vietnam from Frontier to Secondary Emerging Market status, effective on September 21, 2026, subject to a review to ensure sufficient progress has been made in opening up the market to global brokers. These reforms are likely to increase market participation and, in turn, drive demand for derivative products.

A new Ministry of Finance circular came into effect in June 2025 to streamline the registration, custody, clearing and settlement of securities transactions, including derivatives. Meanwhile, a VN100 Index Futures contract was launched in October. The contract, which is the second equity index-based derivative product, covers 95% of the VN All Share Index’s market capitalization.

Looking Ahead

Asia’s growing importance on the global economic stage looks set to drive significant growth in its derivatives market. A combination of growing internal demand from an increasingly sophisticated institutional and retail investor base, combined with regulatory reforms to open up the region’s markets to foreign capital, could potentially contribute to both higher trading volumes and greater product innovation.

Original Post

data.title: DigitalBridge: An Acquisition Could Be A Good Deal For SoftBank And Investors Alike
data.description:
Introduction

DigitalBridge Group, Inc. (DBRG) has been in the news recently due to rumors that a company heavily invested in the AI growth story is looking to take it private to help with its grand project. I wanted to go through the story in more detail and give some comments on the whole situation.

If you are already invested, I would hold off from buying or selling right now, as the rumors are getting quite interesting with many good price points that the acquirer could offer. I wouldn’t gamble on it if I didn't already have any skin in the game, mainly because I am already heavily weighted in AI plays.

Potential Acquisition

Just a few days ago, the company’s downward momentum on its share price was broken due to a potential acquisition by SoftBank Group Corp. (SFTBY). Bloomberg reported that the two companies are in advanced talks, with the aim of taking DBRG private, amid surging AI-driven demand for digital infrastructure, especially growth sectors like data centers. It is still in very early talks, as neither of the companies is willing to comment on the situation, but apparently, according to sources close to the matter, the deal might come through at the end of the year, which is just a few weeks from now. On what seems like a rumor, DBRG’s stock price rocketed 46% on around 56m shares traded, which is around 10 times the average volume.

A Bit about SoftBank

SoftBank’s CEO has been very prominent in the investing community. Masayoshi San pivoted the company aggressively to AI sometime back in 2023, after its Vision Fund posted losses for a whole year straight. The two funds suffered substantial losses of over $80B due to many bets on unprofitable startups during the low-interest rate euphoria back even before COVID-19 took the world by storm. Companies like WeWork, which was its biggest investment failure, had to be written down by over $10B. SoftBank was all in on such startups right before the pandemic. WeWork, the Uber IPO (UBER), and DiDi (DIDIY), all suffered dramatic losses due to the pandemic, forcing Masayoshi to take the loss and pivot. It seems that the man just got very unlucky; nobody could have predicted a pandemic.

Since then, the company has pivoted hard into AI, with massive bets on Nvidia (NVDA), although the company did sell its full early stake for a profit of around $3.3B back in 2019; however, it has missed $150B since then, as the share price went from around $35 to $140. Finally, after many years in the red, the company turned a profit in FY24.

SoftBank is very heavily involved and could say all in on the new growth story, which is undeniably the main talk of the Street, and that is AI and data centers.

Why SoftBank wants DBRG?

The rationale behind this move is rather simple. DBRG is operating in a very hot sector right now. The hottest sector since the dotcom bubble and fiber optics. And actually, the company is still involved heavily in cell towers and fiber optics to accelerate AI infrastructure. By acquiring DigitalBridge, SoftBank essentially gains instant access to its 20.0GW power capacity and the many established data centers, like Vantage’s $25B Texas Campus, massive fiber network, and $108B AUM portfolio. This way, SoftBank will be able to bypass many years of regulatory hurdles, grid connection, and unnecessary costs to build it all from scratch. That would have taken years, literally, and it seems a lot riskier than just buying the company.

Synergies

Since the company is all in now, there are a lot of synergies from buying DBRG. Masayoshi San and the company launched a massive project back in January of this year, in partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI), Oracle (ORCL), and MGX, called Stargate, which targets $500B investment in U.S. AI infrastructure. Masayoshi San is at the helm of the project and is aiming to build data centers in Texas for OpenAI. This is a massive project that is going to span at least four years. Here lies the first synergy point. I mentioned DBRG has a stake in Vantage’s data center, Texas Campus, which comes with a 1.4GW pipeline. Why does that matter? The Stargate Project saw some delays in the area due to insufficient capacity to support the energy requirements; therefore, DBRG’s hyperscaler contracts and fiber networks will enable GPU deployments at the Stargate sites, like the one in Abilene, TX, which is its main location. As DBRG has a 20.9GW pipeline, it provides the project with an instant bank of power. Without the company, that would have most likely meant a 3-5 year waiting time to achieve such scale.

The unprecedented scale that DBRG garnered through years of infrastructure investments in exactly what SoftBank is looking for is very appealing and much more efficient than building from scratch. What kind of costs are we talking about here? Well, apparently, the Stargate Project requires 7GW of power and around $400b of investment, so DBRG’s investment in Vantage’s data center Texas Campus 1.4GW pipeline comes at a massive discount and could cover 20% of the total power needs of the project, for a fraction of the price.

Many analysts came out with their price ranges for how much the company could acquire DBRG for. JPMorgan said it could be sold for as little as $25 a share, and that is quoted as a conservative price, with up to $35 a share upside. That is still a massive premium even after the rumors began circulating, as the company is trading at around $15 a share now. Wells Fargo sees at least $18-$20 a share, which is a lot more conservative, while RBC Capital raised its target price to $23 a share. So, let’s say we take $30 a share as the buy price; that’s double the DBRG’s market cap, bringing it to around $5B. $5B for access to 1.4GW of power is much better than around $57B ($400B investment for 7GW).

This is going to benefit SoftBank, OpenAI, and Oracle tremendously, as it could mean bypassing grid delays that have been plaguing the project since the beginning.

It seems like a no-brainer acquisition.

Briefly on DBRG’s Recent Performance

The company reported fee-related earnings of around $93m, which translates to around 22% y/y growth, so still very healthy. One odd thing we can see on the company’s financials is a massive, carried interest fee reversal of around $120m, which brought down its GAAP revenue this quarter to just under $4m. Carried interest is based on the performance of the funds above specific hurdle rates, which are in the range of 6%-8%, so if the fund doesn’t go above the range of returns, the company has to give back some of that accrued interest, which in this case amounted to $120m.

How you should look at this is to essentially ignore it and focus on fee-related earnings. The company’s Fee-Earning Equity Under Management or FEEUM reached $40.7B, up 19%, and earnings were up 43%, which is very impressive. Carried interest, or performance fee, is nice, but it is not always guaranteed. What is always guaranteed is the 1%-2% fee on FEEUM. So, the company’s core operations are growing and very healthy.

Risks and Closing Comments

The deal is far from done. None of the parties confirmed this rumor as of yet, which means it is pure speculation for now. Only the people close to the two companies have any idea what is happening or what is not happening. What should you do? I think if you have already owned the company for a while during this quite underwhelming performance over the last 5 years (down 22%), I don’t see a reason not to hold on for now to see where this drama leads, if anywhere.

The company’s portfolio is fantastic, focusing on the main growth stories in the modern era, and big tech is increasing its AI-related capex spend dramatically in 2026, which means there is a lot of money to go around, so just follow the money.

I am not going to be buying right now since the rumor is not fully confirmed, which means if it does not come to life, I expect to see a correction once again. If that does happen, I would consider the company a lot more attractive; however, I am quite heavily weighted into the AI growth story, so even then, I would probably avoid it.

But if you don’t have enough exposure to AI somehow, I would say DigitalBridge Group would be a good company to invest in even if it doesn’t go private and the deal doesn’t happen.

data.title: Capital One: What's (New) In Your Wallet
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the RGA Investment Advisors Q3 2025 Investment Commentary.

We recently added shares of Capital One (COF) to our portfolios. At one point, through our shares in ING (ING) years ago, we owned COF indirectly. This resulted from ING's divestiture of ING Direct to Capital One, in exchange for cash and shares totaling just shy of 10% of the company. This acquisition gave Capital One a low-cost, digitally native (branchless) deposit base with a state-of-the-art user interface and consumer experience. Though we never owned COF directly, we watched it admirably for years. When COF announced their Discover (DFS) acquisition, we thought it was too good to be true and feared regulators could prevent the tie-up from happening, but we did our work as we watched to see what would happen. As luck would have it, regulators eventually approved the deal and we now own COF directly.

The acquisition of DFS is a transformative transaction that uniquely positions COF to generate substantial value, fundamentally redefine its competitive standing, and reshape the US payments landscape. By integrating DFS's proprietary network and customer base, COF is shifting from being primarily a card issuer to a vertically integrated payments entity, akin to an American Express (AXP)-like operation with scale rivaling the largest payments networks in the world.

The most immediate and transformative value driver of the acquisition is the ownership of the Discover (DFS) network, which allows COF to avoid regulatory constraints that cap interchange fees for most large banks. This network capability is a "game-changer" that provides an immediate boost to fee revenues by repapering and issuing debit cards to all existing COF customers.

Specifically, the acquisition allows COF to leverage a key exemption related to the Durbin Amendment, which typically caps debit interchange fees, by issuing debit cards on its owned network and negotiating rates directly with merchants. Experts estimate that this switch will result in a pickup of approximately 75 basis points (BPS) in debit interchange rates, leading to an implied weighted average rate of 1.25% on a $50 transaction, compared to the existing Durbin-capped rate of 0.49%. This ability to monetize its massive debit volumes is projected to deliver over $1.0 billion in network synergies by 2027. COF will invest considerably in its merchant network, especially abroad, so that its customer value can truly rival what the Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) (MA) networks offer their own debit customers. As it currently stands, COF is well prepared to do so.

We think it is less likely, though possible that COF eventually pursues this strategy on the credit side of things. Even if they do not go all-in, as they are in debit, there are levers the company can pull to capture greater economics in the credit card network. As it stands, this acquisition elevates COF's scale, making the combined entity the largest credit card issuer in the country and the second largest by transaction volume. Even if COF keeps the credit card setup as is, owning a network and added degrees of scale provides real leverage in future renewal negotiations with Visa and Mastercard.

Central to realizing this value is Capital One's sophisticated technological infrastructure and operational expertise. COF has historically made substantial investments in its tech stack, designed specifically to grow through acquisition and convert new portfolios efficiently onto its platform. This technological playbook is crucial for integrating Discover (DFS)'s systems, especially on the loan and asset management side, where integration is a core competency for COF. This integration capacity drives confidence in achieving significant operational benefits. COF has guided toward realizing approximately $1.5 billion in annual expense savings within two years, representing about 23% of Discover's operating expenses.

Redefining Capital One and Enhancing Its Banking Franchise

The merger facilitates COF's redefinition by broadening its consumer base and strengthening its banking foundation. Discover's portfolio includes prime and student credit customers, complementing COF's traditional strength in the "main street, new to credit" and subprime markets. This shift improves the pro forma loan book mix by directing it toward less consumer subprime risk. The combination with Discover's lower-loss loan book is expected to act as a dampener in future regulatory stress tests. Furthermore, the consolidation eliminates a key top competitor in the main street credit card segment, reducing new customer acquisition costs.

On the banking front, the combined institution would rise from 9th to 6th among US banks by deposits. Discover's own historical strength in online savings combines nicely with COF's online bank that emerged from the ING Direct acquisition over a decade ago.

In preparation for the merger, both COF and DFS accumulated large excess capital balances, resulting in substantial capacity for capital returns to shareholders. To that end, COF recently increased its dividend by 33% and announced a new $16 billion share repurchase program. We expect at least $10b in repurchase activity (or over 7.5% of market cap at today's valuation) each of the next two years, with capacity to either continue repurchases at an aggressive rate or to grow the dividend, depending on how cheap or expensive shares trade.

The translation of these strategic advantages and capital return into shareholder value makes COF an attractive investment opportunity today. The DFS acquisition is more than a simple consolidation; it is a strategic metamorphosis. Taken together, this strategic vertical integration transforms COF into a major payments player and the company is now one of the most important domestic entities in this key space within the financial sector.

The traditional driver of value for banks is tethered to the company's balance sheet and its ability to profitably underwrite loans. Banks are heavily regulated and as such, it is challenging to earn meaningfully in excess of bank cost of capital, effectively constraining the ability to earn very high ROEs in the space. Some banks, including COF have excelled at earning ROEs consistently above their cost of capital, but few have earned ROEs above 20%. A thriving payments franchise with its return profile driven by network effects, technology and intangible assets that do not exist on a balance sheet can earn ROEs well above 20% and now Capital One owns a scaled, rapidly growing piece of this market.

This convergence of network economics and operational efficiency acts like a flywheel, spinning off immense cash flow that can be continuously recycled to investors through accelerated capital returns, while growth in net income accelerates. We expect the combined entity to earn $25 or more in EPS come 2027, versus 2024's $14 in EPS. Most importantly, we expect the bank's Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE) to exceed 20% by 2027, justifying a meaningfully higher multiple in the market than today's 12x trailing and 10x forward P/E.1 Look at a group of comparable P/Es on this year's earnings:

Ticker P/E FY 2025
BAC 13.65
C 13.14
WFC 13.32
GS 16.11
MS 16.35
AXP 22.32
JPM 15.02
COF 10.35
— —
Peer Average 15.03
Average Excluding COF 15.7
ROE Advantaged Average 17.45

The average peer trades right around a 15x P/E. The ROE advantaged group that have earnings streams untethered to the balance sheet trade at nearly 17.5x. At $25 in 2027 EPS, the stock is simply mispriced: even a pedestrian 15x multiple implies ~70% upside, and a hybrid bank / payment-network multiple implies a double.

Jason Gilbert, CPA/PFS, CFF, CGMA, Managing Partner, President

Elliot Turner, CFA, Managing Partner, CIO

Footnotes

1 Pinegap - AI Platform for Equity Research

data.title: The Day Google Became An AI Stock
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the RGA Investment Advisors Q3 2025 Investment Commentary.

We have owned shares in Google (now Alphabet (GOOGL)) since the very first day of 2012. At the time, the perceived threat from the mobile app world caused its P/E ratio to fall to a historic low. The current AI saga feels like "deja vu all over again", with Alphabet's trailing-twelve-month (TTM) P/E setting a new all-time low, even below the 2012 level.

Since initiating our position early in the firm's history, Google has become our largest holding, even as the journey has included its share of difficult stretches. No one stretch has been harder to hold through than this latest stretch, as the stock faced dual overhangs from both the AI threat and the anti-trust action brought by the US government. The anticipation, and eventual arrival, of regulatory action weighed heavily on Google, both competitively and in the market. Investors feared drastic outcomes, including the loss of Apple (AAPL)'s default search placement or even a forced separation of Chrome from the core business. This tension ended on September 2, 2025, when Judge Amit Mehta handed down his ruling on the remedies in United States v. Google LLC.6

While some constraints were imposed, the worst-case scenarios were removed. Internally, we declared this “The Day Google Became an AI stock,” given how Google could largely continue business as usual and more importantly, how the company could absolve key personnel of time committed to this case and redirect the entire focus of the organization to winning in AI.

The AI Evolution of Search

The notion that Google's job is to distribute clicks misstates the company's purpose. Users want answers, not blue links, and Google has been conceptualized and in some respects, architected, for an answer-first world since inception. The very first version of Google in 1998 had an "I'm feeling lucky" button, designed to take users directly to an answer:

From day one, the company was moving toward "zero-click searches," where the search engine provides the answer directly.

Constraints imposed by regulatory actions, beginning with the acquisition of ITA Software (the backbone of travel sites like Expedia (EXPE) and Kayak), required Google to out-license data and avoid suppressing competitor links. Without these restrictions, Google could have used that data to deliver a fully integrated, best-in-class travel experience directly within search. More recently, Yelp (YELP)'s lawsuit against Google underscores the ongoing tension between preserving a fair and open internet in its role as the dominant search engine and optimizing the platform to give users the most seamless, high-quality answers, quickly and directly.7

That all changed with the launch of OpenAI. Not only did Google face a new rival, but the company seemingly faced an existential threat, with the value of search itself in peril. This threat offered Google cover from a fast-moving competitor to build a product geared at answers first and the cover broadened as multiple competitors stepped into the fold. Eventually, not only did Gemini (Google's AI Model) become a powerful standalone product, but Google was also able to weave AI-driven answers into search itself.

The world increasingly insisted that answers, rather than a series of links, was the goal and arbiter of “winning” in AI. Judge Mehta acknowledged this directly in his ruling on the remedies in the case, declaring that “The emergence of GenAI changed the course of this case” given how experts perceive it as a “nascent competitive threat.”8

The Vertical Integration Advantage

Google's long-term investment in AI and machine learning is deep. The key breakthrough that led to the creation of OpenAI—the paper "Attention is All You Need"—was written by the Google Brain team.9 Investments in AI, such as Google Brain/DeepMind and Waymo, were often "masked" by the company's "Other Bets" structure. However, the strategic decision to shift DeepMind's reporting to Alphabet's corporate costs in Q1 2023 reflected the central role of GenAI to the company's entire corporate strategy.

Another key investment is the Tensor Processing Unit (TPU), Google's proprietary chip designed specifically for AI. With the launch of the 7th generation, a meaningful portion of Google's products now run on the TPU. This gives Google an advantage: while competitors must wait for Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs, Google can order its own chips at a substantially cheaper price.

Google now has a distinct and accelerating advantage in AI. It is the only fully vertically integrated AI company, excelling in all five critical areas:

  • Semiconductors—Proprietary TPU chips.
  • Hardware—Owns its datacenters, ensuring needed capacity for itself and capacity to sell to others.
  • Models—Google's Gemini appears to be a top-tier model for many critical functions.
  • Consumer—Dominance in search and Android provides built-in customer relationships and awareness at no incremental marketing spend.
  • Enterprise—Google Cloud Platform (GCP) has a large and growing customer base and can package models with pure computing power.

Competitors check at most three of these boxes. Google's dominance in all areas makes it the low-cost provider for each one individually. This is particularly valuable in Enterprise solutions, where bundling computing power with models is becoming essential. We believe the market perception of Google is shifting from a slow-moving business facing disruption to a dominant and advantaged leader in the technology that will power the next decade.

Jason Gilbert, CPA/PFS, CFF, CGMA, Managing Partner, President

Elliot Turner, CFA, Managing Partner, CIO

Footnotes

6 https://www.cnbc.com/2025/09/02/google-antitrust-search-ruling.html

7 Yelp sues Google, alleging a search engine monopoly that promotes its own reviews | CNN Business

8 https://cases.justia.com/federal/district-courts/district-of-columbia/dcdce/1:2020cv03010/223205/1436/0.pdf?ts=1756893273

9 Attention is All You Need and How Google gave the key breakthrough technology for ChatGPT to OpenAI

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Reliable Cash Flows, Moderate Expectations
data.description:
Following my last article on Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), the stock price has increased by 14% and has outperformed the benchmark. In addition to this, it has exceeded my $188 price target, currently trading at approximately $200.

The company's top-line growth appears to be accelerating, and that is promising. The bottom-line expansion remains steady, but given its recent 40% year-to-date rally, I think the market has partially priced that in, and upside remains capped.

My neutral rating remains unchanged. If we look at the company's performance, the healthcare heavyweight has made significant progress over the past year. I also believe that its business strategy to spin off its healthcare-related consumer product business to a new entity named Kenvue (KVUE) was the right decision.

Now, JNJ positions itself for future success by shifting its core focus to innovative medicine and a MedTech portfolio. However, the revenue growth remains insignificant, reflecting a below-industry growth rate. In addition to this, the capital structure remains leveraged, and that clouds the outlook.

The company remains a stable, reliable, and defensive diversification opportunity, but I think it is more likely to present market-like performance than outperform the benchmark. I anticipate stock price appreciation mirroring bottom-line growth. Therefore, I still rate Johnson & Johnson a Hold with a $220 price target over the next year, indicating 10% upside potential.

Q3 2025: Double-Beat Reflecting Accelerating Revenue Growth

On October 14, the healthcare giant delivered Q3 2025 results. This was a solid quarterly performance, reflecting top- and bottom-line expansion and business stability. The market reacted to the results with a roughly flat performance over the following day, indicating 0.17% appreciation in the stock price.

Johnson & Johnson posted $23.99 billion in revenue, representing a 7% growth on a year-over-year basis, and approximately $238 million above analysts' estimates. The bottom line arrived at $2.80 in diluted EPS, a 16% growth over the past 12 months, and $0.04 above what the market had anticipated.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted that operations ran well on all cylinders. The healthcare giant achieved top-line growth across both businesses.

The worldwide innovative medicine segment has remained its leading revenue contributor, indicating $15.6 billion in Q3 2025, accounting for 65% of the company's total revenue. The sales surged 7% on a year-over-year basis, driven by a strong pipeline across oncology and neuroscience.

The oncology segment posted $6.5 billion in revenue and experienced 21% growth on a year-over-year basis. The immunology segment, although the second-highest contributor in the innovative medicine segment, delivered $4.17 billion in revenue but underwent a 10% decline on a year-over-year basis. Management highlighted that one of the reasons was due to Stelara's decline because of biosimilar competition.

On the upside, the neurology business achieved $2 billion in revenue, a 15% growth on a year-over-year basis. I think investors should monitor whether Stelara's drag continues. It does partially contribute to my neutral stance, as if the decline accelerates, the company's performance may weaken. Nevertheless, despite a significant decline in the segment, Johnson & Johnson achieved solid momentum in demand, highlighting strength in the drug pipeline. Management indicated that 11 brands delivered double-digit growth, and that argues for a bullish continuation.

The worldwide MedTech business generated $8.4 billion in sales, delivering a 7% growth on a year-over-year basis. It consists of technology and devices within the specialties of cardiovascular, surgery, and vision.

The surgery category remained the highest contributor to the top-line performance of the segment, indicating $2.5 billion in revenue, yet only 4% growth on a year-over-year basis. The cardiovascular segment remained the fastest-growing, reaching $2.2 billion in sales and a 13% surge over the past year. The orthopedics posted $2.3 billion in sales, a 4% increase, and the vision category underscored $1.4 billion in sales, representing an 8% growth on a year-over-year basis.

Although the segment presented a solid but single-digit increase versus the same quarter last year, I would like to emphasize that all categories were steadily growing. This argues for a strong market position, business stability, and robust performance. However, if the top-line growth doesn't resume at a double-digit pace, it may be challenging to justify significant premium expansion from these levels.

The slide above underscores that Johnson & Johnson remains well-diversified globally, too. The US remains the leading region with $13.7 billion in revenue, presenting a 6% growth on a year-over-year basis. Europe achieved an 11% surge over the past year, reflecting $5.4 billion. This was also the fastest-growing region for the company, indicating solid momentum and strengthening of its market position across different European countries. I believe such diversification appears compelling and justifies the statement that JNJ remains a high-quality company with significant global presence.

The slide above highlights that the innovative medicine segment experienced roughly $3.1 billion in cost of production sold, implying an 8.5% growth over the past 12 months. This reflects that the segment would have experienced a margin decline, as costs have grown more rapidly than the company's revenue. However, because the leading healthcare company posted a 30% drop in R&D costs over the past year, signaling a decrease from $4.2 billion to $2.9 billion, this resulted in bottom-line growth.

Nevertheless, the company will have to elevate R&D costs; otherwise, I argue, it would be difficult to maintain its stance in the space. Also, this meaningfully supports my neutral stance, suggesting that top-line growth didn't directly translate into bottom-line expansion, and if R&D costs increase in the future, margins may be pressured.

Although the MedTech segment experienced a surge in revenue, implying a 7% revenue growth, this was offset by a meaningful cost increase. It has risen by 15% versus Q3 2024. I argue this is the main concern looking forward, as if the segment's costs continue to outweigh the revenue, this could result in stressed bottom-line growth. It's also likely that such performance would weigh on profitability and margins, and I think that it would be challenging to justify significant multiple expansion from these levels.

Therefore, both of the segments experienced an increase in costs, and that is an issue. Also, in general, I would say that this quarterly report presented a mixed picture. Headwinds tied to Stelara remain, alongside a rise in production costs. I believe that long-term investors should monitor whether this remains a temporary hiccup, as otherwise this could potentially be a more structural issue for JNJ. In case this materializes, this would signal business deterioration, and I believe it may meaningfully stress the stock price.

Fairly Valued And Positioned For Market-Like Performance

I kindly invite you to read my previous article on Johnson & Johnson, providing a more in-depth analysis of its valuation and dividend. However, I would also like to highlight a few things.

The company is trading at a forward P/E of about 18.40, a fair valuation supported by only a 3% difference from the sector median of 19x. The forward P/E chart below also signals that the current earnings multiple presents a multi-year valuation, which also contributes to my thesis of a capped upside.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) basis of 2.64, the healthcare company actually trades at a 47% premium versus the sector median of 1.80. If the company's performance weakens, the valuation metric suggests that the downside is not protected.

In addition to this, on forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 5.48 and 5.14, respectively, Johnson & Johnson trades at a 30% premium at the midpoint versus peers at 3.93 and 4.22. This indicates that the market anticipates strong top-line growth, and it may already be priced in the stock. Moreover, I am not sure whether solid single-digit growth may be enough to justify premium expansion from these levels.

The company's leveraged capital structure is arguing for consolidation. It has nearly $46 billion in total obligations, and although it has $18.56 billion in cash, its outlook remains clouded. On the upside, on a TTM basis, JNJ experienced $206 million in net interest income, highlighting that its obligations are tied to favorable conditions. The company benefits from high-yield accounts for its high cash reserves on the balance sheet, and that covers interest expenses.

If the Fed continues its easing cycle, the company's interest income may drop, and this could also cloud bottom-line growth. This remains an area to monitor for investors.

Over the past year, Johnson & Johnson achieved only 5% revenue growth, and that reflects an underperformance versus peers at approximately 6.5%. If management will not succeed in resuming top-line expansion, it would support my neutral stance, and it may be challenging to justify stock price appreciation.

I kindly invite you to read my previous article with a more detailed analysis. Nevertheless, to sum up, Johnson & Johnson continues to dominate the healthcare space and remains a leading company in the industry. That may argue for a premium valuation, alongside revenue and bottom-line growth. However, I believe the current valuation indicates a fair price, and unless it unlocks a new revenue growth cycle, it is difficult to justify premium expansion from these levels.

Wall Street analysts forecast $10.87 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $11.52 in diluted EPS for FY2026. This indicates solid, but single-digit bottom-line growth, and I agree with the expectations. I also expect stock to appreciate in price following a diluted EPS increase, but single-digit performance may not be enough to meaningfully outperform the benchmark. Thus, if we apply a 19x earnings multiple, a slight premium over current due to premium business, solid market position, and high growth of a few innovative medicine portfolios, to the FY2026 EPS of $11.52, we arrive at my price target of $220. This supports my neutral position, arguing for a stock price increase but market-like performance.

My Verdict: Johnson & Johnson Is A Hold

I believe that Johnson & Johnson could be an interesting consideration for long-term investors who favor a stable business that has a strong market position and a well-recognized brand. In addition to these traits, JNJ also offers a defensive approach and dividend income to its shareholders. However, given its recent rally, the stock reached a fair valuation, and without unlocking new catalysts, I would not be surprised if the stock consolidates for a while.

Risks and concerns remain, as I have indicated above and in my previous article. A single-digit top and bottom line growth may not be enough to justify premium expansion. Stelara's performance may continue weighing on the performance, and that could meaningfully affect investor sentiment. Also, execution risks tied to pipeline and regulatory scrutiny could affect the stock price, and if sentiment in the broader market shifts, JNJ may correct along with other names. It presents a mixed picture, which argues for consolidation.

I rate Johnson & Johnson a Hold with a $220 price target over the next year. This presents a 10% upside possibility, but I don't think it will meaningfully outperform the benchmark from these levels. 25 Wall Street analysts imply a $204 price target, highlighting a 2% upside potential.

data.title: RGA Investment Advisors Q3 2025 Investment Commentary
data.description:
The Great Divide: Why the Stock Market is So Concentrated and Where We Are Finding Opportunities

In the third quarter, the market continued and extended its recovery from the Liberation Day crash. Increasingly, the “market” as we have come to speak of it, has been dominated by a handful of mega cap companies. Consequently, the equal-weighted S&P 500 (NYSE: RSP (RSP)) is a far more accurate proxy for how the median stock is performing, and the divergence between RSP and SPY (SPY) shows just how distorted the cap-weighted “market” has become. This dynamic was highlighted in a recent Wall Street Journal column by Spencer Jakab titled “Stocks are Up–But Maybe Not Your Stocks.” This performance divide reflects the S&P 500’s extreme concentration within a small subset of the largest companies, as seen below:1

Magnificent Performance of equal and capitalization-weighted S&P 500 funds since 2023

The S&P 500 (SPY) is experiencing a historic level of concentration; the top 10 companies now account for over 40% of the index's total weight:2

Exhibit 28: Concentration of S&P 500 market cap and earnings in the 10 largest index constituents

This level of concentration is unusual and makes historical comparisons of metrics, such as Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratios, less relevant than they typically would be. This also creates performance challenges for active investment strategies, which often focus on smaller and mid-cap stocks and tend to underweight the market's largest names.

Tensions in The Market: Speculation and Ai Focus

Alongside this concentration, we are observing two concurrent forces creating unique market tensions:

1. High Retail Participation and Speculation: Retail speculation is now strong enough to bend valuation logic as pockets of the market are trading at levels that have little to do with fundamentals and everything to do with narrative. Generally, stocks with faster revenue growth should justify a higher Enterprise Value to Sales ratio (EV/S). While most stocks cluster around a line of best fit, there are notable exceptions, such as Palantir (PLTR), whose high multiple is difficult to rationally justify.3

2. AI Dominance: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is "sucking the air out of everything else." Even outside the top 10 stocks, many market winners have a connection to AI. This is evident in the top 10 holdings of the Russell 2000 small-cap index, which include AI infrastructure and quantum computing stocks. Several of these smaller companies lack revenue, and those that do have sales boast some of the highest EV/S ratios in their sectors and the market at large.4
